coredata.dc:title: Insights on salting-out triggering aggregation-induced emission in nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ayala-Fonseca, Alfredo
Ramírez-García, Gonzalo
Mota-Morales, Josué D.
Bugallo, Andrés De Luna
De la Rosa, Elder
Salas, Pedro
coredata.dc:description: A straightforward methodology for obtaining Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) using salting-out purification processes is presented in this work. Various salts, including NaCl at concentrations of 50 mM, were found to be effective for stratification of graphene-based materials (GBMs) by salting-out treatments. These salts were shown to be safer, more cost-effective, and environmentally sustainable alternatives to (NH4)2SO4 reported in previous works. Remarkably, this method demonstrated the ability to promote the self-assembly of N-GQDs into stable J-aggregates-like arrays with an average fluorescence decay time of ca. 4 ns and absolute quantum yield with values of 3.5 % and 6.9 % for 100 mM and 2 M, respectively. In contrast to a typical quenching effect caused by π-stacking, these arrays exhibited aggregation-induced emissions, as evidenced by the presence of new excitation and emission bands at 532 and 600 nm, respectively. The scalability of the process, along with the absence of additional purification steps, renders these results of great scientific and technological interest. Furthermore, the modulable fluorescence, size, and stability of these GBMs suggest their potential application in energy cells, sensors, and biomedical imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salting-out
Graphene quantum dots
Purification
N-GQDs
Aggregation-induced emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the cations (NH4 +, Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+)
Aggregation-induced emission in N-GQDs triggered by salting-out

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Centro de Investigación y de Estudios Avanzados del Instituto Politécnico Nacional

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Programa de Doctorado en Ciencias e Ingeniería de Materiales

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend special thanks to María Priscila Quiñonez Angulo for her excellent technical assistance in NMR. Luz María Reyna is acknowledged for their support with DLS and zeta potential measurements at CINVESTAV Querétaro. Elsa Nydia Hernández Ríos and Mario Flores Salazar are recognized for their assistance in the acquisition of fluorescence microscopy and AFM images. Luis Alfredo Ayala Fonseca is a doctoral student from the “Programa de Doctorado en Ciencias e Ingeniería de Materiales” at the “Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM)” and acknowledge the fellowship from CONACYT ( CVU:909908 ). Project with the support of program PAPIIT IN101023 .

The authors extend special thanks to María Priscila Quiñonez Angulo for her excellent technical assistance in NMR. Luz María Reyna is acknowledged for their support with DLS and zeta potential measurements at CINVESTAV Querétaro. Elsa Nydia Hernández Ríos and Mario Flores Salazar are recognized for their assistance in the acquisition of fluorescence microscopy and AFM images. Luis Alfredo Ayala Fonseca is a doctoral student from the “Programa de Doctorado en Ciencias e Ingeniería de Materiales” at the “Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM)” and acknowledge the fellowship from CONACYT (CVU:909908). Project with the support of program PAPIIT IN101023.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Emission spectra (A) and maximum emission graph (B) of the salting-out dispersions of N-GQDs with (NH4)2SO4, NH4NO3, NaNO3 and NaCl at concentrations of 10, 25, and 50 mM. Maximum emission of salting-out N-GQDs dispersions after treatments using NaCl and NaNO
3
at concentrations of 5–1000 mM (C). All under 395 nm excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Emission spectra (A) and emission maxima (B) of the N-GQDs after salting-out treatments with NH4NO3, LiNO3, NaNO3, and KNO3 50 mM, exciting at 395 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Emission spectra of the dispersions obtained by salting-out using 25 mM NH4NO3 (A) and LiNO3 (B); (C) shows the shift of wavelength peak of signal emitted for dilutions 1:2, 1:4 and 1:8 after excitation at 395 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Emission spectra of the N-GQDs dispersions obtained from NaCl treatments at concentrations of 50, 500, and 1000 mM, immediately after treatment (A) and after ten days of rest (B), using an excitation source of 395 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–vis absorption spectra of salting-out treatments with NaCl at concentrations of 50, 500, and 1000 mM, and a dispersion of NaCl 500 mM blank, after the rest (10 days). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Emission spectra of the N-GQDs dispersions obtained from the salting-out treatments with NaCl at 10, 25, 50, 50, 500, and 1000 mM, using an excitation source of 532 nm (A), photograph of the N-GQDs dispersions (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram proposed for energy shift in H-aggregates (ACQ) and J-aggregates (AIE) of GBMs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

DLS size distributions of N-GQDs obtained using salting-out treatments with different concentrations of NaCl: 10 mM (A), 50 mM (B), 500 mM (C), and 1 M (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
AFM images and heigh profile of GBMs obtained using salting-out treatments with NaCl 10 mM. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fluorescence microscopy images of N-GQDs dispersions with AIE obtained using salting-out treatments with NaCl 50 Mm(A) and NaCl 1000 mM (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectra of salting-out treatments with NaCl at concentrations of 50, 25, and 10 mM (A), D and G band zoom, and ID/IG index (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Ionic radius (r) and electronic charge density (z/r) of cations and anions [19].

head.ce:title: Insights on salting-out triggering aggregation-induced emission in nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Qro, 76230, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Fisica Aplicada y Tecnologia Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, Qro., 76230, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facultad de Ingenierías y Tecnologías, Universidad La Salle Bajío, León, Gto, 37150, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Facultad de Ingenierias y Tecnologias, Universidad La Salle Bajio, Leon, Gto. 37150, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward methodology for obtaining Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) using salting-out purification processes is presented in this work. Various salts, including NaCl at concentrations of 50 mM, were found to be effective for stratification of graphene-based materials (GBMs) by salting-out treatments. These salts were shown to be safer, more cost-effective, and environmentally sustainable alternatives to (NH4)2SO4 reported in previous works. Remarkably, this method demonstrated the ability to promote the self-assembly of N-GQDs into stable J-aggregates-like arrays with an average fluorescence decay time of ca. 4 ns and absolute quantum yield with values of 3.5 % and 6.9 % for 100 mM and 2 M, respectively. In contrast to a typical quenching effect caused by π-stacking, these arrays exhibited aggregation-induced emissions, as evidenced by the presence of new excitation and emission bands at 532 and 600 nm, respectively. The scalability of the process, along with the absence of additional purification steps, renders these results of great scientific and technological interest. Furthermore, the modulable fluorescence, size, and stability of these GBMs suggest their potential application in energy cells, sensors, and biomedical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small 0D graphene fragments, which due to quantum confinement and edge effects, exhibit distinctive properties such as modulable photoluminescence and slow relaxation of hot carriers [1]. They show great potential in sensors and bioimaging applications due to their tunable properties that depend upon surface functionalization and size, such as excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and stability at physiological conditions [2,3]. GQDs present some advantages over pristine graphene and other graphene-based materials (GBMs) due to basal planes with fewer defects and more accessible edges, which can be decorated with a large number of specific functional groups, facilitating the charge transfer between different target molecules and GQDs. In addition, GQDs overcome important limitations of other GBMs such as low colloidal stability and a strong tendency for aggregation [2,4].

Most of the existing techniques for obtaining GBMs on a large-scale result in populations that are highly heterogeneous in size and surface functionalities. Therefore, the development of methodologies for the purification and control of the chemical and morphological characteristics of these materials, and particularly in GQDs, represent a current challenge. Salting-out is a purification methodology that is currently of great importance in biology, health sciences, and related industries for purifying and isolating proteins and nucleic acids due to its operational simplicity and high throughput character [5]. However, separation via salting out has been scarcely studied for obtaining GQDs, systematically testing only (NH4)2SO4 as the ionic agent. GBMs, such as graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs, present important similarities to proteins in many aspects, like great heterogeneity of sizes similar to the wide range of molecular weights of proteins, amphiphilic character, and variable electronic and optical behaviors according to their size and chemical functionalities, which is more evident when the size of the GBMs approaches the Bohr radius and GQDs are obtained [5,6]. In this regard, Ryu et al., in 2014 reported that Na2SO4 and other ammonium salts such as acetate, chloride, and nitrate were comparatively ineffective for the purification of GQDs from graphene oxide (GO) aqueous dispersions. The overall outcome showed the same trends as the Hofmeister series, which is commonly used as a reference in studies with proteins. However, there was a lack of evidence regarding the behavior of the alternative salts, except for photographs showing varying degrees of macroscopic aggregation in the dispersions. Subsequently, a handful of works have been published about this methodology for the purification of GQDs and, N-GQDs, although only focusing on (NH4)2SO4 [5–8].

Recently, Ozyurt et al. reported the obtention of GBMs that exhibits similar behavior to the J-aggregates of dye molecules, which consists in a bathochromic shift associated with the phenomenon known as AIE (aggregation-induced emission). Aggregation stands as a primary challenge in controlling the properties of GBMs, and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in GQDs is a well-known effect that limits their applications [9]. However, reports regarding the modulation of GQDs properties, including light emission as well as the manipulation of the mechanisms underlying the formation of such aggregates, are still very scarce. J-aggregates of dyes (also called Scheibe aggregates) are characterized by a narrow absorption band that is shifted towards longer wavelength (bathochromic shift) compared to the absorption band of the monomer. Additionally, they exhibit an increasing fluorescence with a narrow band and a very small Stokes shift [10]. The spectral shifts in emission band are in many cases easily recognizable (≈100 nm) and have been associated with new excited states being formed by extended domains of coherent coupled dipoles. This phenomenon is considered an archetypal example of exciting emerging properties resulting from the self-assembly of systems at the supramolecular or nanoscale level [9–11]. GBMs like N-GQDs with AIE which present longer-distance exciton coupling, are candidates to replace many common dyes, paving the way for applications such as sensing, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, fingerprints recognition and solar concentrators [12–15].

Herein, we report a study on the purification of N-GQDs by an advantageous salting-out process, which yields N-GQDs with a narrow window of physicochemical properties like AIE, thermal stability, high concentration of dispersions, and resistance to photobleaching. Obtaining these materials from the salting-out method was performed using different salts such as NaCl, Na3C6H5O7 (trisodium citrate), and different nitrates, which represent readily accessible and safe alternatives to the more commonly reported (NH4)2SO4. Additionally, it was studied the excitation and emission in the visible region of N-GQDs caused by the AIE phenomena, which can be modulated by controlling their aggregation using a facile salting-out methodology with NaCl.

High purity graphite rods (Spectro-Grade) with ash content <2 ppm, were acquired from Electron Microscopy Science (Hatfield, Pennsylvania, USA). KNO3, H2SO4 and HCl were purchased from J. T. Baker (Radnor, Pennsylvania, USA). KMnO4, LiNO3, NaNO3, MgNO3⋅6H2O, and CaNO3⋅4H2O in powder and H2O2 30 % (w/w) in H2O were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Chemical reagents were used without further purification.

Emission spectra were obtained with a Silver-Nova 200 spectrometer with a 395 nm UV light source SL1-LED from StellarNet Inc. (Tampa, Florida, USA), and a 532 nm laser source from Laserglow Technologies (Ingram, North York, Canada). Atomic force micrographs were obtained using a Bruker (Billerica, Massachusetts, USA) Innova AFM in contact mode with SiN tips. The samples were deposited on silicon wafers, then dried under vacuum, excess material was removed using distilled water and allowed to dry again. Raman spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker Senterra Raman spectrometer with a 785 nm laser and 4 cm−1 resolution. FTIR was recorded with a Bruker Vector 33 using a diamond ATR cell, 4 cm−1 band resolution, and 1064 nm source. In both cases, Raman and FTIR, the samples were measured as powder dried at 65 °C. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (1H) was carried out on a Bruker Avance III Ultra Shield 400 MHz spectrometer at room temperature (30 °C). The samples were dried at 65 °C for 48 h and redispersed using d-chloroform as solvent. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific spectrometer Genesys 10 S (Waltham, Massachusetts, USA). DLS and zeta potential were measured with a Zetasizer Nano ZS from Malvern Panalytical (Malvern, Worcestershire, UK), using a 632.8 nm laser. Fluorescence decay time and absolute quantum yield (QY) were carried out by using fluorometer FS5 from Edinburg. Decay time were measured at 510 nm upon excitation with 405 nm from a 70 ps pulsed laser. QY were measured exciting a 395 nm from a xenon lamp and using an integrating sphere of 14 cm diameter. Fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using a Nikon (Minato, Tokyo, Japan) Eclipse E−600 phase contrast and fluorescence upright microscope, which has an HB-1010 lamp (12 V, 100 W, tungsten-halide lamp) and three filters configurations (Excitation: 330–380 nm, Dichroic Mirror: 400 nm, Barrier Filter: 420 nm), (Excitation: 450–490 nm, Dichroic Mirror: 505 nm, Barrier Filter: 520 nm) and (Excitation: 510–560 nm, Dichroic Mirror: 565 nm, Barrier Filter: 590 nm).

Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained by the well-known modified Hummers' method and can be reviewed in our previous work [16,17]. Stable heterogeneous dispersions of GBMs functionalized with nitrogen groups were obtained by means of a simple one-pot methodology based on ultrasound treatments in ammonium hydroxide to generate a simultaneous fragmentation and functionalization of the GO sheets. For this, 300 mg of GO were dispersed in a volume of 30 ml of 15 % NH4OH and placed the dispersion in an ultrasonic bath for 3 h. Then, 270 ml of distilled water were added to obtain a dispersion of 1 mg/ml of GO and NH4OH 1.5 %. The 300 ml of the new dispersion was sonicated for 3 more hours and then centrifuged for 15 min at 30,000 G. Finally, the stable dispersions of N-doped GBMs were separated from the precipitated material and stored them in closed flasks labelled as NGA.

For the purification treatments by salting-out, 10 ml of the stable GBMs dispersion (≈0.5 mg/ml) were placed in 15 ml falcon tubes with the desired concentration of salts and shaken manually for a few seconds. Then, samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 30,000 G, and dispersions separated from the precipitated material. FTIR spectra of graphenic precursors after each treatment (GO, NGA and NGA precipitated material), as well as the N-GQDs obtained through salting-out, are presented in Figures SI1 and SI.2, respectively.

To obtain the N-GQDs from the suspension NGA, the salting-out methodology was tested first using ammonium sulfate (NH4)2SO4. As mentioned above, this salt is widely used in protein purification due to its position within the Hofmeister series, where the high ionic strength of the ammonium cation and the sulfate anion play significant roles in the salting-out process. The efficiency of (NH4)2SO4, i.e., separation of optically active N-GQDs from GBMs dispersion, was compared with three compounds of lower cost (NH4NO3, NaNO3 and NaCl) at concentrations of 10, 25, and 50 mM. Positive results were achieved in all treatments with a concentration higher than 10 mM, regardless of the salt used. Fig. 1 A shows the emission spectra of the dispersions obtained from these treatments, using a 395 nm excitation source. In these samples, an emission band centered around 510 nm is evident for the four salts ((NH4)2SO4, NH4NO3, NaNO3 and NaCl) at concentrations of 25 mM and 50 Mm. Among these treatments, the dispersion treated with NH4NO3 at 50 mM exhibits the highest emission intensity, while the NaCl 10 mM displays the lowest (null emission). Fig. 1B shows the emission maxima of the N-GQDs dispersions obtained in the different treatments, revealing a non-linear yet increasing trend in the emission intensity of the dispersions as the concentration of salts increases, which is normal for this type of treatment.

To assess the effectiveness of the treatments, the maximum emission of the dispersions was used as an indicator of the concentration of optically active GBMs (N-GQDs) obtained through purification. Those results already suggest that the ionic strength of the dispersion is not the sole determining factor in the purification of N-GQDs through salting-out. Specifically, the efficiency of the treatments using (NH4)2SO4 at concentrations of 25 and 50 mM is lower than the corresponding concentrations of NH4NO3, despite (NH4)2SO4 contributes three times more to the overall ionic strength compared to NH4NO3. NaCl, on the other hand, showed the lowest effectiveness, although very close to NaNO3 and (NH4)2SO4 at the 50 mM concentration. In the case of the treatments with a concentration of 10 mM, the almost negligible emission is caused by attributed to the ineffective separation of the N-GQDs from the rest of GBMs. In such case, the larger material absorbs a significant portion of the light emitted by both the excitation source and the N-GQDs, as depicted in the photograph inset of Fig. 1B, corresponding to the salting-out treatments with NaCl 10 and 50 mM.

Based on the positive initial outcomes utilizing sodium nitrate and sodium chloride, an optimal concentration was evaluated by extending the range of analysis to concentrations between 5 and 1000 mM. Fig. 1C displays the emission maxima generated by the treatments with sodium chloride and sodium nitrate at concentrations of 50, 100, 250, 500, and 1000 mM under 395 nm excitation. Notably, in both instances, the 50 mM concentration produced the most favorable outcomes (highest emission intensity). However, higher concentrations exhibited a gradual decline in performance due to the successive precipitation of luminescent material. This outcome may be due to the potential for a stratified separation of the optically active N-GQDs based on their size or degree of functionalization, favored by the higher ionic strength of the treatments, as previously reported [5,6]. Sodium nitrate shows superior performance compared to chloride in all dispersions, although very close to the optimal concentration of 50 mM. A comparison with sodium citrate treatments ranging from 50 to 1000 mM displayed a similar pattern as sodium nitrate, although a weaker performance, despite it contributing more to the overall ionic strength, see Figures SI3 and SI.4. This observation emphasizes the importance of the specific interactions between ions, solutes, and medium, as discussed in resent research on salting-out mechanisms [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the cations (NH4 +, Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+)
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of LiNO3, NaNO3, and KNO3 were compared to those of Mg(NO3)2 and Ca(NO3)2 at concentrations of 10, 50, and 100 mM to analyze the impact of cation charge. At concentrations higher than 10 mM, the divalent cations demonstrated the capacity to precipitate all the particles of GBMs including those optically active. This resulted in transparent solutions that did not exhibit fluorescence when exposed to the UV excitation source (395 nm) (Figure SI5A). On the other hand, colloidal dispersions obtained with the monovalent cations at low concentrations (10 mM) were not stable over time. These cations proved to be effective in purifying the N-GQDs, causing the larger particles of GBMs to precipitate after 10 days of rest at room temperature. Furthermore, the monovalent cations presented colloidal dispersions particle with higher emission levels than those presented for divalent ones, as evidenced in Figure SI5B. It is worth noting that following this ten-day resting period, the various dispersions exhibit colloidal stability and the AIE-related phenomena, which will be discussed below.

The 50 mM concentration was found to be an optimal concentration for the obtention of N-GQDs in all the salting-out treatments using salts with monovalent cations, starting from a heterogeneous dispersion of GBMs (0.5 mg/ml, 1.5 % NH4OH). Optimal concentrations were identified individually for each salt within the range of 50–100 mM (Figure SI6). However, the performance differences among the treatments within this range were found to be relatively small. From the different treatments and parameters analyzed, we found the following series of anions and cations according to their efficiency for the purification of N-GQDs by the salting-out technique, which differs from the Hofmeister's series. However, it follows a trend that can be correlated to the charge density of the cations and the effect of specific interactions.
Anions: NO3 − ≈ SO4 2− > C6H5O7 3− > Cl−
Cations: NH4 +>Li+>Na+>K+>Mg
2+ ≈Ca
2+

The effectiveness of NaCl treatments is of particular interest because of their low cost, toxicity, and reactivity, which together with the characteristics of the salting-out technique could allow their large-scale implementation. Furthermore, the presence of these ions within biological systems and the biocompatibility of N-GQDs suggest a facile translation to applications in life sciences such as bioimaging. Finally, the use of high concentrations of NaCl shows evidence of being more effective in stratify the optically active material (Fig. 1C), which could contribute to the AIE phenomenon discussed in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation-induced emission in N-GQDs triggered by salting-out
ce:section.ce:para: The first indications of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) in the obtained N-GQDs coincide with those of J-aggregates of dye molecules initially described by Scheibe et al. [20] and Jelley [21] independently. That is, upon dilution with distilled water, the emission band maximum of N-GQDs was slightly shifted toward lower energy wavelengths (bathochromic shift). Fig. 3 C shows the emission maxima of the dispersions obtained by salting-out using two of the best treatments previously identified (Fig. 2B), namely NH4NO3 and LiNO3, at 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, and 1:8 dilutions. Their emission spectra are presented in Fig. 3. A and 3. B, respectively. A gradual shift from 513 to 528 nm was observed in the case of LiNO3, with a nearly linear trend of approximately 5 nm per dilution. Similarly, in the case of NH4NO3, the shift ranges from 514 to 533 nm. In these spectra, it can be also observed that a second band around 610 nm becomes more evident as the dilution factor increases. Both, bathochromic shift and second emission, in addition to colloidal stability, are observed in all samples after a rest period of 10 days.

Derived from the purification treatments of N-GQDs by salting-out, the bathochromic shift of the emission and excitation bands of the material can be associated with the aggregation of the particles after a resting time, which is explained by the AIE phenomenon. This aggregation is a consequence of the high ionic strength of the dispersions and the combination of electrostatic repulsion and enhancement of the hydrophobic effect that governs the salting-out processes and disfavors the fully solvated states of particles, leading to aggregation.

The bathochromic shift of N-GQDs dispersions was most noticeable in the presence of high concentrations of NaCl, as reported for J-aggregates of dye molecules [10]. Interestingly, this effect is less pronounced with NaNO3, which showed higher efficiency in N-GQDs purification by the salting-out treatments, see Fig. 3. In addition, NaCl presents a series of advantages concerning its implementation and storage and facilitates the analysis of the material through spectroscopic characterization techniques such as Raman and FTIR, as well as NMR. Fig. 4 A shows the emission spectra centered at 510 nm obtained from NaCl treatments at concentrations of 50, 500, and 1000 mM, and Fig. 4B the corresponding spectra of the N-GQDs dispersions after ten days of rest, using an excitation source of 395 nm. The treatments with 500 mM and 1 M NaCl, lead to the emission splitting into two bands located at 510 and 575 nm after 10 days in suspension. In the case of the 50 mM NaCl treatment, there is only a shift of the maximum from 510 nm to 520 and a shoulder around 550 nm. Inset of Fig. 4B shows a photograph of N-GQDs dispersions in NaCl 50 mM and 1 M, excited with 395 nm after 10 days rest.

Quantum dots resulting from salting-out treatments with 100 mM and 2000 mM NaCl after 10 days of rest presented quantum yields (QY) of 3.5 % and 6.9 %, respectively. These results confirm the relevant role of the salt concentration in inducing AIE phenomena and they are consistent with the observed quenching of the emission band centered at 510 nm and the increase in intensity at 575 nm, as shown in Fig. 4B. These experimental results, along with the absorption spectra presented in Fig. 5 , confirm the formation of an intermediate energy level as a result of N-GQDS aggregation, which in turn leads to AIE. The presence of such level is further supported by the analysis of fluorescence decay time. Decay time was measured for samples treated with 50 mM, 500 mM, and 1000 mM NaCl after 10 days of rest, as shown in Figure SI7 and Table SI.1. In all cases, the data was fitted using three exponential functions with τav ∼4, 4.5 and 4 ns, respectively. Interestingly, the rapid decay time shows a significant increase that correlates with the NaCl concentration. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that this decay corresponds to a non-radiative process, populating the new energy level responsible for the 575 nm emission.

The absorption spectra of these N-GQDs dispersions are presented in Fig. 5. It is observed a band around 560 nm in the N-GQDs dispersions obtained with the salting-out treatments at concentrations of 500 mM and 1 M, which has not been reported as characteristic of GBMs such as GQDs, GO or graphite. However, a similar bathochromic shift and AIE phenomenon in CQDs (carbon quantum dots) was reported in 2013 b y Gao et al. [13] Therein and subsequent reports, such shift was attributed to strong π-π stacking interactions in the aggregates. This new absorption band observed in the N-GQDs treated with 500 and 1000 mM NaCl, extends from 500 nm to 575 nm. Notice the shoulder in the range between 510 and 520 nm which coincides with the maximum of the first emission band observed on sample treated with 50 mM NaCl (Fig. 4A). This overlapping can lead to energy transfers between the dispersed N-GQDs and the aggregates under 395 nm excitation.

Fig. 6 Shows the emission spectra of the N-GQDs dispersions obtained from the salting-out treatments with NaCl at 10, 25, 50, 50, 500, and 1000 mM, using an excitation source of 532 nm, and the corresponding photographs. The 500 mM and 1 M treatments show a well-defined emission band with a maximum of around 600 nm, which differs from the maximum observed in the spectra obtained using UV light (395 nm). The asymmetry of the emission band centered at ∼600 nm is a consequence of the filter used. The difference in the intensity of this phenomenon, at high concentrations, is shown to be directly related to the increase in the size of the aggregates according to the DLS results.

The aggregation-induced emission in GBMs observed in dispersions after salting-out treatments is a scarcely reported phenomenon to date. However, the characteristics of these materials, as well as their behavior resembling that of J-aggregates of dye molecules and CQDs, allow us to theorize about the mechanisms that give rise to the phenomenon, see Fig. 7 . In the molecular exciton theory, such π–π stacking phenomena are categorized as H-aggregates and J-aggregates and involve the AIE and ACQ (aggregation-caused quenching) phenomena. The prevalence of AIE over ACQ is considered to arise from the restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) in an aggregated state, due to the fact that these physical restrictions block the non-radiative relaxation pathways, promoting the excitons to decay radiatively [22]. On the other hand, AIE is highly dependent on the structure of the chromophore and the electronic interactions arising from the packing. According to Wang et al. direct-bond electronic conjugation forms the structural basis of AIE luminogens, and the crystal lattice of graphene formed by a large number of aromatic rings can give rise to a large π-conjugation electronic system, similar to that observed in many of the J-aggregates of dye molecules, which could endow these materials with AIE activity [23].

Fig. 8 shows the histograms with the size distributions versus number of particles in the dispersions of N-GQDs after the NaCl treatments at concentrations of 10, 50, 500, and 1000 mM (1 M). In the case of the lowest concentration of salt corresponding to 10 mM (Fig. 8A), the treatment yields three populations of GBMs with hydrodynamic diameters of around 23, 197, and, 916 nm, whose populations represent 33.4, 64.8 and, 1.8 % respectively. In the case of the 50 mM concentration (Fig. 8B), considered optimal for the purification process, we found that a single group of N-GQDs with a diameter around 28 nm was obtained. The difference in size between this population and that observed with the 10 mM treatment (23 nm) may be associated to the increase in the concentration of NaCl ions leading to a greater effect of aggregation due to ionic strength of the dispersion. This would confirm that the 50 mM concentration turns out to be efficient for the purification process of N-GQDs by salting-out by allowing to isolate them from the larger GBMs. On the other hand, the treatments with higher NaCl concentrations (500 and 1000 mM) also show a unique group around 379 and 461, respectively (Fig. 8. C and 8. D). Additionally, SI.8 shows the DLS results as a function of intensity and the Z-potential of these dispersions in SI.9. These results suggest that the 50 mM concentration can be considered optimal for the purification of N-GQDs and for the obtention of a unimodal particle size distribution. In the treatments with higher concentrations (500 and 1000 mM), the average particle size increases along with the concentration, which correlates with the observed AIE phenomenon.

Fig. 9
shows atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the GBMs obtained by Salting-out using NaCl 10 mM. It is observed the presence of populations similar in size to the hydrodynamic ratio determined by DLS (Fig. 8), which were centered around 916, 197, and 23 nm. Fig. 9A shows that indeed the most abundant group corresponds to materials with dimensions close to 200 nm and the presence of larger sheets with dimensions of about 1 μm, although the smaller aggregates (23 nm) cannot be observed at this scale. On the other hand, Fig. 9B and 9c shows the presence of a considerable number of smaller-sized sheets, as well as evidence of the splicing of sheets of sizes below 40 nm, whose coupling could be giving rise to the larger GBMs and the AIE. Additionally, Fig. 9D and 9E shows the 3D projection of image 9. C, and the corresponding height profile. These indicate that the lateral dimensions of the smaller GBMs are between 20 and 40 nm, while height differences of between 1 and 3 nm could indicate the presence of GBMs within the few-layer category [24,25]. Images of the treatments with higher salt concentrations could not be obtained due to the formation of crystals during the drying of the samples.

Fluorescence microscopy images of N-GQDs dispersions obtained using 50 and 1000 mM NaCl were acquired and are presented in Fig. 10. A and 10. B respectively. The images were obtained using three excitation ranges, one in the UV region (330–380 nm, DM: 400 nm, BA: 420 nm) and two in the visible region, blue (450–490 nm, DM: 505 nm, BA: 520 nm) and green (510–560 nm, DM: 565 nm, BA: 590 nm).

From this type of microscopy, it is not possible to determine the size of the N-GQDs and their aggregates, however, the images obtained allow us to identify two different populations of particles or aggregates. The first group of small particles only shows fluorescence when excited with ultraviolet light (330–380 nm). A second group with sizes exceeding 10 μm, in addition to emitting blue when excited with UV light, was shown to be excitable with visible light, with an emission in the red when excited between 510 and 560 nm, which would coincide with the emission spectra and the other techniques that suggest the phenomenon of aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Additionally, it was observed that the aggregates of this second group presented green emission when excited between 450 and 490 nm, like what was observed using a 395 nm source.

Raman spectra of the materials obtained with the NaCl treatments at concentrations of 10, 25, and 50 mM are presented in Fig. 11 . The Raman spectra of the treatments with higher concentrations could not be obtained due to high background fluorescence induced by the 532 nm laser of the Raman system. In the spectra of Fig. 11A the two main characteristic bands of the GBMs are observed. The D band around 1320 cm−1 is associated with defects or vibrational modes corresponding to carbon atoms with sp3 hybridization, while the G band around 1573 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of the sp2 carbon atoms that form the characteristic hexagonal lattice of the GBMs. Additionally, the 2D (2600 cm−1) and D + G (2900 cm−1) bands corresponding to the characteristic overtones of these materials are observed. Fig. 11B Shows a close-up of the two main bands of the GBMs spectra normalized to the D band, where it can be observed a shift to higher wavenumbers of the G band. There is also an increment of intensity in the N-GQDs spectrum corresponding to the salting-out treatment with 10 mM NaCl with respect to those of 25 and 50 mM. The characteristics of this G-band are often used to determine the number of layers (stacked sheets) in GBMs. The aforementioned differences in the 10 mM NaCl treatment, red shift and ID/Ig decrease (Fig. 11B), could be associated with a higher number of layers than in the 25 and 50 mM dispersion materials [26]. Although DLS analyses show that treatments using NaCl at different concentrations result in significant changes in the hydrodynamic diameter of GBMs (Fig. 8), Raman spectra allows to confirm the graphitic nature of the material, and do not indicate a significant increase in the number of layers caused by increasing the salt concentration in the treatments and aggregation-induced emission occurs. This instead suggests that the aggregation does not occur in a conventional way, probably avoiding quenching due to π-stacking. Together with the AIE evidence presented above, it can be inferred that this is due to differences in the aggregation process of these materials and the formation of J-aggregates that respond to the ionic strength of the dispersions.

1H NMR was used to further characterize the chemical structure of the N-GQD. Recently, NMR has emerged a useful technique to identify side products (e.g. organic dyes and other contaminating fluorophores) that affect the study of the intrinsic properties of CQD, being particularly common in bottom-up synthesis methodologies [8,27]. Figure SI9 shows the 1H NMR spectra of the materials obtained by salting-out treatments with 1 M, 500, and 50 mM NaCl. These spectra show broad aliphatic signals corresponding with the formation of CNDs, GQDs, or high molecular weight carbon particles and polydisperse polymers [28]. On the other hand, some of the additional bands observed are associated with amides and other nitrogenous functional groups in this type of material. This observation correlates with the functional groups observed by FTIR presented in Figures SI1 and SI2, which resulted of the GO fragmentation treatments with ammonium hydroxide.

The use of a top-down methodology such as the utilized in this work, starting from a graphitic precursor, hinders the possibility of preferentially obtaining unwanted side-products. Most reports on obtaining GQDs by oxidative cleavage and similar methodologies, suggest the formation of CO2 and other highly volatile species as the main by-products. Therefore, the AIE phenomenon is associated unequivocally to the structure and functionalization of the obtained GBMs. The salting-out is then an advantageous purification method for the separation of GQDs produced through top-down strategies.

In this study, salting-out resulted in an effective method for separating N-GQDs from highly heterogeneous GBM dispersions. In addition to the classic (NH4)2SO4, various salts were also assessed, exhibiting excellent performance. A concentration of 50 mM was found to be optimal for all the treatments with salts containing monovalent cations examined. On the other hand, divalent cations (Mg2+, Ca2+) present a better performance at low concentrations (<10 mM) due to their charge density. Different series of anions (NO3 − ≈ SO4 2− > C6H5O7 3− > Cl−) and cations (NH4 +>Li+>Na+>K+ >Mg2+≈Ca2+ were defined according to their efficiency for the purification of N-GQDs by the salting-out technique. The trend observed can be correlated to the charge density of the cations, along with possibly specific interactions in the case of NH4 +. NaCl is thereby introduced in this work as an outstanding alternative for salting-out due to its widespread availability, low environmental impact, toxicity, cost and reactivity, safe storage, and large-scale production.

Remarkably, after using salting-out treatments, the analysis of morphology and structure through DLS, AFM, and Raman techniques suggests N-GQDs aggregation occurring in a non-conventional manner, preventing quenching caused by π-stacking. In addition, dispersions of N-GQDs obtained via salting-out treatments exhibit a J-aggregates-likes behavior. Based on the results from fluorescence microscopy and the emission spectra of the N-GQDs dispersions, evidence of the aggregation-induced emission triggered by salting-out in these materials is provided. Therefore, salting-out presents an effective methodology for purification and control of the AIE phenomenon through a simple variation of the ionic strength of the media. These findings can pave the way for the industrial-scale production and purification of N-GQDs, thereby promoting their future applications.

Alfredo Ayala-Fonseca: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Gonzalo Ramírez-García: Supervision. Josué D. Mota-Morales: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Andrés De Luna Bugallo: Writing – review & editing. Elder De la Rosa: Writing – review & editing. Pedro Salas: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend special thanks to María Priscila Quiñonez Angulo for her excellent technical assistance in NMR. Luz María Reyna is acknowledged for their support with DLS and zeta potential measurements at CINVESTAV Querétaro. Elsa Nydia Hernández Ríos and Mario Flores Salazar are recognized for their assistance in the acquisition of fluorescence microscopy and AFM images. Luis Alfredo Ayala Fonseca is a doctoral student from the “Programa de Doctorado en Ciencias e Ingeniería de Materiales” at the “Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM)” and acknowledge the fellowship from CONACYT (CVU:909908).
